checks.a_analysis: I have compared the text content of the output JSON against the source PDF. Every sentence in the output paragraphs and headings corresponds directly to text found in the source document. There is no hallucinated body text or invented instructional content.
The source document does not contain any phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs. The output JSON also contains no contact information, correctly reflecting the source material.

The source document consists of 3 pages of instructions. The output JSON contains sections and elements corresponding to all text and images from all 3 pages, specifically covering the initial setup (page 1), the security key entry and portal acceptance (page 2), and the success confirmation (page 3).
There are no tables in the source document or the output document.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: A careful comparison shows that all instructional text within each section is fully captured. No paragraphs or sentences have been truncated or omitted.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output matches the source almost exactly. Minor changes such as the normalization of the curly apostrophe in 'SSID’s' (source) to a straight apostrophe in 'SSID's' (output) are acceptable reformatting. No wording changes were found that alter the meaning of the instructions.

checks.a_analysis: The output document does not contain any duplicated text blocks or repeated sections. Each paragraph and heading from the source appears only once in the corresponding JSON section.

The output uses an H1 for the main document title, which is appropriate. The remaining instructional text blocks are correctly identified as paragraphs rather than headings, reflecting their functional role in the source document. No heading levels are skipped.
The source document does not contain any tables; therefore, no table structural defects are possible.

The output document has significantly altered the reading order compared to the source procedural guide. In the source PDF, text instructions are interleaved with illustrative screenshots (Step 1 Text -> Step 1 Image -> Step 2 Text -> Step 2 Image). In the JSON output, all text elements for a given page/section are grouped together at the beginning, followed by a group of all images for that section. This breaks the logical association and instructional flow essential for a user guide.
checks.b_evidence_source: On Page 1, the instruction 'Click on Computer Icon in bottom right of screen to open options' is immediately followed by a screenshot of the taskbar tray. Page 2 follows the same interleaved pattern for its three steps.
checks.b_evidence_output: In 'sections[0]' (Page 1), reading_order elements 2 through 5 are text paragraphs, while elements 6 through 9 are the images that correspond to them. Similarly, in 'sections[1]', elements 10-12 are text followed by elements 13-15 which are images.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the document, specifically elements in sections[0], sections[1], and sections[2].

checks.a_analysis: While the source text describes a sequence of steps, they are presented as standard paragraphs rather than formatted bulleted or numbered lists. The output correctly reflects this as a sequence of paragraph elements.
The source document does not contain any functional links or visible URLs. No links were injected or omitted in the output.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the converted document is significantly different from the source PDF, negatively impacting the document's utility as a step-by-step guide. In the PDF, each instruction is immediately followed by its corresponding screenshot. In the HTML, multiple instructions are grouped together, followed by a group of images. More critically, the sequence of images and captions for the Wi-Fi connection process (entering the security key and the verifying screen) is swapped and mislabeled in the HTML. Specifically, the 'Verifying and connecting' image appears under the instruction for the security key, and the security key prompt image appears under the instruction for the verifying screen. This rearrangement breaks the logical visual flow and confuses the meaning of the instructions.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2 of the PDF shows a clear chronological sequence: Text instruction for 'enter the network security key' is followed by the security key entry box image, which is then followed by text for the 'verifying and connecting screen' and its respective image.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows several instructions grouped together. The 'Verifying and connecting' image is rendered before the security key image. Furthermore, the caption 'Popup screen for NCGov-Guest network security key' is placed under the 'Verifying and connecting' image, and the caption 'Verifying and connecting screen' is placed under the security key entry image.
checks.b_evidence_location: The sequence starting from 'Once NCGov-Guest SSID is selected...' to the 'Login successful' screen.

checks.a_analysis: There are no tables present in the source PDF, so there is no table rendering to evaluate.

All nine meaningful screenshots and icons found in the source PDF are present and rendered correctly in the HTML output. While their placement is incorrect (as noted in Check V1), no meaningful image content is missing.

There are no instances where genuine body content blocks (paragraphs, tables, or images) are duplicated in the HTML. The 'Login Successful' and 'Connected' images appear only once in the body, although out of order. The extra text at the bottom of the screenshot appears to be visible alt-text or OCR data for accessibility purposes, not a duplication of original body text.
